title: Apple and Audi Alumni Have Made a Luxe EV Based on the Moon Buggy
gist: Amble, a Lisbon-based startup founded by Cowboy ebike veterans and alumni from Apple, Audi, and Ford, is launching the $25,000 Amble One — a street-legal, doorless EV buggy targeting the premium hospitality and second-car micromobility markets. With 500+ vehicles committed and over €10 million in signed revenue from luxury resort clients, the company is pursuing a top-down go-to-market strategy before expanding to urban consumers, with a follow-on 'Amble Two' platform planned for 2029.

insights.claim: Amble is deliberately entering the micromobility market through premium hospitality clients rather than urban mass-market consumers, citing the failure of prior urban-first micromobility ventures as validation for this approach.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A top-down luxury-to-mass strategy may be the only viable path in micromobility, where urban-first startups have repeatedly failed to build sustainable businesses.
insights.quote: A lot of companies in micromobility start in the urban market and want to compete with everyone, and we all know that this did not work out so far.

insights.claim: Amble has secured 12 signed hospitality clients, over 500 vehicles committed, and more than €10 million in signed revenue before its public launch, with first deliveries to properties beginning mid-2027.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Pre-launch revenue of €10M+ from luxury resort clients provides a credible early proof-of-concept for premium EV micromobility before broader consumer rollout.
insights.quote: The company apparently has 12 signed clients, over 500 vehicles committed, and more than €10 million in signed revenue.

insights.claim: The Amble One targets the segment of second cars owned by families, arguing that the majority of secondary vehicles are unnecessarily expensive and that a purpose-built, short-range EV priced at $25,000 could displace them.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: If the second-car replacement thesis holds, the addressable market for sub-$30K EVs in developed markets is far larger than current micro-EV incumbents suggest.
insights.quote: Most families do not need twice that $50,000 BYD or Tesla. The second vehicle for families could be something that is designed for purpose, designed for shorter trips.

themes.theme: Regulatory Engineering as Product Design Constraint

insights.claim: Achieving the EU L7e vehicle classification — which allows public road use without full car regulation — requires a curb weight under 450 kg, a constraint Amble's CEO describes as 'really hard' and one that drove the open, doorless design of the Amble One.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The 450 kg L7e threshold is a hard regulatory moat: meeting it while delivering perceived-premium quality is a genuine engineering barrier that limits competition in this segment.
insights.quote: To qualify as an L7e vehicle in Europe...the Amble One must stay under 450 kilograms. 'This is really hard,' says Adrien Roose, CEO and cofounder.

insights.claim: The Amble One's doorless, open design is not purely aesthetic but is a functional consequence of meeting the EU L7e sub-450 kg weight limit, illustrating how regulation directly shapes EV form factor in the light mobility segment.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Investors in European light EV startups should model regulatory weight thresholds as a binding constraint on design; firms that ignore this face either full car homologation costs or non-road-legal products.
insights.quote: The Amble One's open, doorless design isn't just an aesthetic choice...It's part of what helps make the weight target achievable.

themes.theme: Founder Pedigree and Design Philosophy Transfer

insights.claim: Amble's design lead Julian Hoenig previously worked on Apple's canceled Project Titan car program and contributed to the Apple Watch and Vision Pro, and is applying Apple's material-first design philosophy — 'pick the material suited to the job, let manufacturing drive the form' — directly to the Amble One.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Ex-Apple industrial design talent embedded in a hardware startup is a rare differentiator; the market for premium EVs is increasingly won on perceived-quality aesthetics, where Apple's methodology is proven.
insights.quote: What did carry over, however, was a philosophy: Pick the material suited to the job, let manufacturing drive the form.

insights.claim: Amble CEO Adrien Roose cofounded Cowboy, one of Europe's most recognized premium electric bike brands, giving Amble a founding team with direct experience scaling a premium electric mobility startup from zero to market recognition.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Cowboy proved that a design-led premium positioning can work in electric mobility against commoditized competition; Amble is attempting to replicate this playbook at a higher price point and in a newer vehicle category.

insights.claim: Amble's chairman José António Uva owns São Lourenço do Barrocal, one of Europe's most acclaimed rural luxury retreats, providing the company with direct access to the exact hospitality-client persona it is targeting as its first customers.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Board-level access to luxury hospitality networks materially de-risks early B2B revenue for a hardware startup in an unproven vehicle category.

themes.theme: Competitive Landscape: Light EV and Supermini Category

insights.claim: Stellantis-owned Citroen's Ami — a 28 mph, 46-mile range supermini EV — is the incumbent in the L7e category, and Stellantis recently announced capacity expansion for its supermini EV line, signaling growing industrial confidence in the segment.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Stellantis expanding L7e production capacity validates the segment's commercial viability and will intensify competition for Amble at lower price points.
insights.quote: Stellantis, which owns Citroen, recently announced plans to expand capacity for its supermini electric cars. 'This is the beginning of a turning point,' Roose says.

insights.claim: The electric Moke is a direct aesthetic and positioning competitor to the Amble One, both targeting open-design coastal/resort EV buyers; Amble differentiates through Apple-trained industrial design talent and a higher sticker price of $25,000.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The premium-doorless-EV niche is no longer a white space; investors should watch whether Amble's design premium justifies a price gap over Moke and Ami for resort operators.

insights.claim: The Slate electric truck — revealed in the same week as Amble's launch — reflects a simultaneous global trend of affordable yet purpose-specific EVs emerging across multiple form factors and price bands.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Multiple simultaneous launches of stripped-down, purpose-specific affordable EVs suggest a product-market fit inflection point in non-sedan EV categories in 2026.
insights.quote: It seems to be the week for cheap EVs. Right after the production model of the Slate electric truck was revealed...a new European entrant in electric mobility is launching out of stealth mode today.

themes.theme: Amble Two Platform and Second-Car Replacement Thesis

insights.claim: Amble's second platform — the 'Amble Two' — is already in design, targets a 2029 release, and will feature removable doors, a lower roofline, and a hardtop, explicitly positioning itself as a replacement for the family's second car rather than its primary vehicle.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The Amble Two represents a larger addressable market than the One; its 2029 timeline means Amble needs the One's hospitality revenue to bridge three years of runway before the mass-market product lands.
insights.quote: A second platform—already in design and targeting a 2029 release—will move further toward conventional-car territory, with removable doors, a lower roofline, and a hardtop.

insights.claim: Amble is structuring its product roadmap around replacing the second car in multi-vehicle households rather than competing for primary car ownership, a deliberate positioning choice that avoids direct competition with Tesla, BYD, and legacy OEMs.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Competing for the second-car slot in affluent households is a defensible niche that sidesteps the brutal price war in primary-vehicle EVs while still addressing a large TAM.
insights.quote: it cleverly aims to replace not a family's primary car, but its second car.

themes.theme: EV Weight Engineering and Materials Innovation

insights.claim: The Amble One uses aluminum, leather, cotton, and cork as primary materials, with large visible orange screws marking all removable or reconfigurable elements — a modular design approach that simultaneously reduces weight and allows field reconfiguration by non-specialists.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Modular, user-reconfigurable EV architecture could reduce total cost of ownership for hospitality fleets that need to adapt vehicles for different use cases (shuttle, golf, cargo) without workshop visits.
insights.quote: Large, friendly orange screws throughout mark every removable or reconfigurable element.

insights.claim: The Amble One weighs under 450 kg and delivers 60+ miles of range with a 5-hour charge from a standard home socket, requiring no dedicated EV charging infrastructure — a spec combination that is particularly attractive to remote luxury resorts.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The ability to charge from a standard socket removes the need for infrastructure investment at remote hospitality sites, lowering the total cost of adoption for resort clients and expanding the addressable install base.
insights.quote: a range of more than 60 miles, a top speed capped at 40 mph, a five-hour charge from any standard home socket, and a curb weight under 450 kilograms.

insights.claim: The Amble One's visible 'skateboard' electric platform — directly inspired by NASA's Lunar Roving Vehicle — is a deliberate design statement that exposes the EV drivetrain architecture rather than concealing it, differentiating the vehicle from conventional automotive design language.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Aestheticizing the EV platform itself (rather than hiding it) is a novel brand-building approach that could resonate with tech-literate early adopters but may limit mainstream appeal.
insights.quote: You see the skateboard. And then we put toppings on it.

insights.claim: Amble's initial B2B clients include Amangiri in Utah, Mustique Island, Six Senses Les Bordes in the Loire Valley, and Na Praia in Comporta — ultra-luxury properties whose guests represent the highest-income consumer segment globally, providing Amble with product-market validation among aspirational early adopters.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Ultra-luxury resort placement functions as paid product marketing to the precise consumer demographic Amble hopes to convert to personal ownership, making the B2B hospitality channel a strategic brand-building investment, not just revenue.
insights.quote: Properties including Amangiri in Utah, Mustique Island, Six Senses Les Bordes in the Loire Valley, and Uva's own Na Praia in Comporta have placed orders.

insights.claim: Amble plans to begin hospitality deliveries in mid-2027 and open consumer deliveries in 2028, meaning the company has approximately 12-18 months of pre-revenue runway after its public launch before cash flows begin.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The 12-18 month pre-delivery gap is a key funding risk: hardware startups with signed orders but no revenue frequently face bridge financing challenges, and investors should assess Amble's current cash position against this timeline.
insights.quote: The first hospitality deliveries of the Amble One begin in mid-2027, while consumer preorders for Europe and the US are now open, with deliveries in 2028.
